versionNotes: Sprint 4 co-design — students help shape Sprint 4 through 4 aspects with individual reflection + partner discussion
title: Sprint 4 Co-Design
description: Help design Sprint 4. For each of 4 aspects, reflect individually, then discuss with a partner. Switch partners after Aspect 2.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 1: What Should We Be Able to Do?

Think beyond specific assignments. After 16 weeks in this course, what should every student be able to do or demonstrate about professional development and self-directed learning? Think about capabilities that would matter to an employer, to your career, to your ability to grow independently.

Instructions: Write your individual response first. You will share and discuss with a partner next.
questions.prompt: What should every student who completes this course be able to do or demonstrate? Name 2-3 specific capabilities — not topics covered, but things you can actually DO.
questions.placeholder: After this course, every student should be able to assess their own skill gaps honestly — not just say 'I need to improve at communication' but identify something specific like 'I avoid asking clarifying questions in meetings because I don't want to look uninformed.' The second capability: being able to find and evaluate information sources in your field independently, not just consume whatever shows up in your feed. The third: being able to explain your learning process to someone else, like in a job interview — not 'I took a class' but 'here's how I identified a gap, built a plan, and tested whether it worked.'

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 1

Turn to the person next to you. Share your response. Listen to theirs. Then discuss together:

  • Where do your answers overlap? Where do they differ?
  • Think about the entire class — would your answers serve everyone, or just people like you?
  • What capability would you add after hearing your partner?
questions.prompt: After discussing with your partner, write down: (1) your partner's name, (2) the capabilities you both agreed matter most, and (3) anything that changed in your thinking.
questions.placeholder: Partner: Kyle Bulloch. We both agreed that honest self-assessment is the foundation — if you can't see your own gaps, nothing else in the course matters. Kyle pointed out something I missed: the ability to give useful feedback to someone else, not just 'good job' but the kind of specific observation that actually helps them improve. That's different from what I listed because it's outward-facing — I was only thinking about what I need for myself. For the class to work, everyone needs to be good at helping others see what they can't see on their own.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 2: What Activities Help Us Get There?

Given what you said we should learn in Aspect 1 — what types of activities would actually develop those abilities? Think about what has worked so far: self-assessments, triad accountability, information diet audits, reflections. What has not worked? Consider both in-class sessions and homework.

Instructions: Write your individual response. Same partner discussion follows.
questions.prompt: What types of activities — in class and as homework — would help develop the capabilities you identified? Name at least one activity type that has worked well this semester and one that has not.
questions.placeholder: The triad accountability check-ins worked well — having to report to two other people about what I actually did (not planned to do) kept me honest. The information diet audit in Sprint 3 was also effective because it forced me to look at what I actually consume, not what I think I consume. What didn't work as well: some of the quizzes felt like comprehension checks rather than reflection. I'd prefer a 5-minute handwritten response to a specific question over a multiple-choice quiz — the writing forces me to think, the quiz lets me guess.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 2

Share your response. Then discuss:

  • Which activities from Sprints 1-3 actually changed how you think or work?
  • Which felt like busywork?
  • If you could design one in-class activity and one homework activity for Sprint 4, what would they be?
questions.prompt: After discussing: (1) your partner's name (same partner), (2) activities you agreed work best, (3) what you would change or add for Sprint 4.
questions.placeholder: Partner: Kyle Bulloch. Best activities: triad check-ins and the in-class peer conversations where we actually talk through our thinking out loud — Kyle said those were the moments where he learned the most because hearing someone else's reasoning exposed gaps in his own. For Sprint 4, we'd want more structured peer feedback — not just 'what did you think of my reflection' but specific prompts like 'what's one assumption in my plan that might be wrong?' We'd also shorten the written homework and add more in-class discussion time.

questions.infoBlock:
Switch partners. Find a different person to sit with for Aspects 3 and 4.

Aspect 3: How Do We Know We Got There?

Sprint 4 ends with a demo and a portfolio defense where you prove your growth across the UMPIRE cycle.

Failing and learning from that experience is also learning. A student who set a goal, missed it, understood why, and adjusted has stronger evidence of growth than one who hit every target without being challenged. The defense should reward honest struggle — not just polished outcomes. How should we assess whether you actually developed the capabilities from Aspect 1? What counts as evidence? What format lets you show your growth most honestly?
questions.prompt: How should we assess your learning — what counts as real evidence of growth? And what defense format would let you show that growth most effectively? Consider: presentations, portfolio walkthroughs, peer challenges, Q&A sessions, or something else.
questions.placeholder: The best evidence is specific before-and-after: 'In Week 1, I rated myself a 4 on self-directed learning but couldn't name one thing I'd learned on my own outside of class. By Week 11, I built a reading habit around AI policy because I realized my career depends on understanding it — and I can point to three specific articles that changed my thinking.' A portfolio walkthrough where I narrate the UMPIRE arc across all 4 sprints would work — showing the messy parts, not just the polished reflections. Q&A from peers would keep it honest.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 3

Share with your NEW partner. Discuss:

  • What kind of evidence would convince a skeptic that you actually grew? Remember: showing what failed and what you learned from that experience is also evidence of growth.
  • Should the defense be 1-on-1 with the instructor, in small groups presenting each other's work, or something else?
  • Think about what would work for the whole class, not just you.
questions.prompt: After discussing with your NEW partner: (1) new partner's name, (2) what evidence format you agreed on, (3) what defense structure would work best for the class.
questions.placeholder: New partner: Anna Chua. We agreed the format should be small groups of 4-5 where each person walks through their growth arc and the group asks real questions — not softball ones. Anna made a good point: the evidence should include at least one thing that didn't go as planned and what we did about it. A student who only shows successes probably isn't being honest. For the class size, small group defenses with peer Q&A feel right — 1-on-1 with the instructor would take too long and miss the benefit of peers catching things the instructor wouldn't.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 4: What Should Sprint 4 Look Like?

Sprint 4 is about preparing for and delivering your portfolio defense. The defense covers your entire journey across the UMPIRE cycle. Given everything you discussed in Aspects 1-3, what should Sprint 4 include to best prepare you?
questions.prompt: What should Sprint 4 look like? What weekly structure would help you prepare for the portfolio defense? What would make the final 4 weeks feel like they matter?
questions.placeholder: Week 13: set Sprint 4 goals and pick the focus area for the portfolio defense. Week 14: build the portfolio — gather artifacts from all 4 sprints, write the narrative that connects them. Week 15: peer review of portfolio drafts in triads — get honest feedback before the defense. Week 16: defense day. What would make it meaningful: the defense should feel like a job interview, not a class presentation. If I can walk out of it with something I'd actually show an employer, that changes everything. The triads should stay — they've been the most consistent accountability structure all semester.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 4

Share with same partner as Aspects 3-4. Discuss:

  • If you designed Sprint 4 for the whole class, what would the non-negotiables be?
  • What should be flexible?
  • One concrete recommendation to Prof. Sathya.
questions.prompt: Final discussion capture: (1) partner's name, (2) your group's top recommendation for Sprint 4, (3) one thing that should definitely stay and one thing that should definitely change.
questions.placeholder: Partner: Anna Chua. Top recommendation: the portfolio defense should require showing at least one failure and what we learned from it — not as a gotcha, but because that's where the real growth evidence lives. Keep: triad accountability and the handwritten reflections — Anna and I both said those slowed us down in a good way. Change: reduce the number of quiz-style assignments and replace them with fewer, deeper reflections tied to specific decisions. Quality over quantity — five honest paragraphs beat fifteen checkbox answers.
